docs.title: BunkerM Documentation
docs.text:
Get up and running fast

Everything you need to deploy, configure, and extend BunkerM. One container, one command.

QUICK START

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

Then open http://localhost:2000 in your browser and set up your first Admin account.

docs.title: What is BunkerM?
docs.text:
BunkerM is an open-source, containerized MQTT management platform. It bundles Eclipse Mosquitto with a full web dashboard in a single Docker container. No manual broker setup, no juggling separate tools.

With BunkerM you get:

  • A production-ready Mosquitto MQTT broker
  • A web UI for managing clients, roles, groups, and ACLs
  • Real-time monitoring and connection logs
  • A built-in MQTT Browser to explore topics and payloads
  • AI-powered assistance through BunkerAI (optional cloud feature)

docs.title: BunkerM vs BunkerAI
docs.text:
BunkerM is the open-source core. It gives you a fully functional Mosquitto MQTT broker with a web management interface. You can create clients, define ACL rules, monitor connections, browse topics, and configure the broker - all from a browser. BunkerM works completely standalone without any cloud dependency or account required.

BunkerAI is an optional cloud-connected layer that adds AI capabilities on top of BunkerM. When you connect your BunkerM instance to BunkerAI Cloud from Settings > Integrations, you unlock:

  • A conversational AI assistant that lets you control your broker in plain language
  • Automated watchers that monitor topics and trigger actions when conditions are met
  • Scheduled jobs that publish messages on a cron schedule
  • Smart anomaly detection and intelligent alerts
  • Multi-channel access via Web Chat, Slack, and Telegram
  • Natural-language ACL management ("create a client called sensor1 with read access to sensor/#")

BunkerAI uses AI to give you full natural-language control over your Mosquitto broker, including ACL management. All BunkerAI features are surfaced in the BunkerM dashboard once you connect. BunkerM remains fully functional without BunkerAI - the AI layer is additive, not required.

docs.title: Basic (data lost on restart)
docs.text:
docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

This is fine for evaluation but your MQTT clients, ACL rules, and web UI accounts will be lost when the container stops.

docs.title: With persistent volumes (recommended for production)
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  -p 1900:1900 \
  -p 2000:2000 \
  -v bunkerm_data:/nextjs/data \
  -v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto \
  -v mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto \
  --name bunkerm \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

What each volume stores:

Volume Contents bunkerm_data Web UI user accounts, BunkerAI settings, agent data, anomaly history, API keys mosquitto_data MQTT persistence files, dynamic security (clients, ACLs, roles, groups) mosquitto_conf Mosquitto broker configuration files

docs.title: With Docker Compose (recommended)
docs.text:
version: "3.8"
services:
  bunkerm:
    image: bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest
    ports:
      - "1900:1900"
      - "2000:2000"
    volumes:
      - bunkerm_data:/nextjs/data
      - mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto
      - mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  bunkerm_data:
  mosquitto_data:
  mosquitto_conf:

Save this as docker-compose.yml and run:

docker compose up -d

See Running BunkerM with Persistent Storage for full details including backup strategies and upgrade procedures.

docs.title: BunkerM Enterprise — Marketing & Ads Strategy Roadmap
docs.text: Context: BunkerM reached 440 GitHub stars and 10k+ Docker pulls organically within the first few months of release. Reddit and LinkedIn have generated inbound contacts but no closed enterprise deals yet. This document outlines a phased, low-budget strategy to move from organic traction to repeatable enterprise sales.

docs.title: Strengths
docs.text:
• Strong organic signal: 440 stars and 10k+ Docker pulls in a few months is genuine product-market fit evidence, not manufactured growth
  • Open-source Community edition creates a self-serve pipeline — people try it before they ever talk to sales
  • Local LLM and AI chat features are genuinely differentiated in the MQTT management space
  • Single Docker container deployment is a strong enterprise selling point (no complex infrastructure, air-gap friendly)

docs.title: The Gap
docs.text:
Inbound contacts exist but are not converting to paid deals. This is almost always a middle-of-funnel problem, not a top-of-funnel one. The internal champion at a company finds BunkerM, likes it, but cannot make the case to management without: - A clear Enterprise offering with defined deliverables - Proof that others are using it in production - Materials they can forward to their CTO or procurement team

Fix the middle of the funnel first. Then spend on ads to fill the top.

docs.title: Target Buyers
docs.text:
Do not try to sell to everyone. Focus on these profiles:

Profile Where They Are What They Need to Hear OT / Automation Engineer LinkedIn, industry forums, PLC/SCADA communities Reliability, air-gap capability, no vendor lock-in IoT Architect at mid-market company GitHub, Hacker News, dev communities Open-source credibility, Docker-native, REST API quality IT / Security Manager in regulated industry LinkedIn, industry press Compliance readiness, audit trail, SOC integration System Integrator / VAR LinkedIn, partner networks Easy deployment for their clients, white-label potential, margins

Priority geography: Western Europe first (France, Germany, Benelux, UK). These markets have strong IIoT adoption, GDPR-driven preference for self-hosted solutions, and shorter enterprise sales cycles than North America for SMB-to-mid-market.

Priority sectors: 1. Manufacturing and industrial automation (largest MQTT user base) 2. Building automation and smart facilities 3. Healthcare and life sciences (air-gap, compliance angle) 4. Agriculture and agritech (offline capability, Raspberry Pi support) 5. Oil, gas, and utilities (regulated, high MQTT usage)

docs.title: Phase 0 — Fix the Funnel Before Spending (Week 1, Free)
docs.text: These actions cost nothing and will make every euro of ad spend more effective.

docs.title: 0.1 — Capture Production Users
docs.text:
Add a passive survey to the BunkerM dashboard (a dismissible banner or a card in the Settings page) that asks: "Using BunkerM in production? Tell us about your setup." Link to a free Tally or Typeform asking: - What sector are you in? - How many MQTT devices are you managing? - What made you choose BunkerM?

This surfaces real testimonials, real use cases, and real company names to use as social proof.

docs.title: 0.2 — Create the Enterprise One-Pager
docs.text: Before running any ad, you need a landing destination that converts enterprise visitors. Create a simple one-page PDF (and a matching section on bunkerai.dev) covering: - What BunkerM Enterprise includes vs Community - Deployment options: on-premises, air-gapped, cloud VM - Security and compliance posture: TLS, ACL, audit trail, SOC-ready integrations - Support terms: response time SLA, dedicated onboarding - Reference deployments (anonymized is fine: "European food manufacturer, 200 devices") - How to get a quote: one button, links to sales@bunkerai.dev

docs.title: 0.3 — GitHub Discussions Activation
docs.text: Post a thread in GitHub Discussions: "Are you running BunkerM in production? Share your setup." This creates social proof visible to every future visitor, surfaces real use cases, and often generates inbound from people who were lurking.

docs.title: Phase 1 — LinkedIn Before Anything Else (Weeks 2-4, Budget: €200-300 test)
docs.text: LinkedIn is more expensive per click than Facebook but your conversion rate for enterprise will be 5-10x higher because you can target by job title, industry, and company size simultaneously. Start here.

docs.title: Campaign Setup
docs.text:
Objective: Lead generation (LinkedIn Lead Gen Forms, not website traffic — removes the friction of a landing page)

Targeting: - Job titles: OT Engineer, Automation Engineer, IoT Architect, Infrastructure Engineer, CTO, VP Engineering, Head of IT, Systems Integrator - Industries: Manufacturing, Oil and Gas, Utilities, Healthcare, Construction and Facilities - Company size: 50 to 500 employees - Geography: France, Germany, Netherlands, Belgium, UK — test one country first (Germany or France) - Language: match the country

Ad formats that work for developer tools on LinkedIn: 1. Single image ad with a dashboard screenshot and a short headline: "Manage 10,000 MQTT devices with one command. Open-source, self-hosted, AI-powered." 2. Short video (60-90 seconds) showing the AI chat controlling a device with plain English — this is the most differentiating visual you have 3. Document ad: share the Enterprise one-pager as a native LinkedIn document — high engagement for technical audiences

Budget recommendation: - Start with €200 over 2 weeks - Set a daily cap of €15-20 - Optimize for Lead Gen Form opens, not clicks - After 2 weeks, evaluate cost per lead. Under €30 per lead is good for this audience. Under €15 is excellent.

docs.title: Content to Run in Parallel (Organic, No Cost)
docs.text: Post these on your personal LinkedIn and the BunkerM page during the campaign period to reinforce paid exposure: - "440 GitHub stars in 3 months — what we learned building an open-source MQTT platform" (milestone post with social proof) - Short screen recording of the AI chat: "Turn off the conveyor belt" being executed with no topic knowledge required - One customer story post, even if lightly anonymized: "A factory in [country] is managing X devices with BunkerM — here is what their setup looks like"

docs.title: Phase 2 — Facebook and Instagram for Retargeting Only (Weeks 3-8, Budget: €50-100/mo)
docs.text: Do not run cold Facebook ads for enterprise B2B software. The audience is wrong and the intent is wrong. Use Facebook exclusively for retargeting people who have already shown interest.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
1. Install the Facebook Pixel on bunkerai.dev (one script tag in the Layout header)
  2. Create a Custom Audience: "People who visited /pricing or /docs/bunkerai in the last 30 days"
  3. Run a retargeting ad with a softer CTA than the LinkedIn lead gen: "Still evaluating BunkerM for your team? Book a 20-minute demo." Link to a Calendly.

This keeps you top of mind for the 90% of visitors who did not convert on first visit. Facebook retargeting to a warm audience (your own website visitors) typically costs €0.50 to €2 per click — very cheap for the quality.

docs.title: Facebook Cold Ads — When to Use Them
docs.text:
Facebook cold ads can work for the Community/hobbyist segment, not enterprise. If you want to grow Docker pulls and GitHub stars faster, a €50-100 Facebook campaign targeting: - Interests: MQTT, IoT, Home Assistant, Raspberry Pi, Docker, self-hosted software - Age: 25-45 - Geography: US, UK, Germany, France, Australia

...will grow top-of-funnel Community adoption, which feeds the enterprise pipeline organically over time.

docs.title: Phase 3 — Content Marketing for Inbound (Ongoing, Free)
docs.text: Paid ads rent attention. Content owns it. Three article types that reliably drive enterprise inbound for infrastructure tools:

docs.title: Type 1 — Sector-Specific Deployment Guides
docs.text:
Write one article per target sector showing a realistic deployment scenario. Examples: - "MQTT management in a food processing facility: traceability, access control, and anomaly detection with BunkerM" - "Smart building automation with BunkerM: managing 500 MQTT endpoints in a commercial property" - "Air-gapped MQTT infrastructure for healthcare IoT: a BunkerM deployment guide"

When an OT engineer at a food company Googles "MQTT management food processing", they find you. This is high-intent traffic that converts without any ad spend.

docs.title: Type 2 — Comparison and Evaluation Content
docs.text: Target people actively in the decision phase: - "Self-hosted MQTT broker management vs cloud IoT platforms: what changes at scale" - "Eclipse Mosquitto alone vs BunkerM: what you gain with a management layer" - "BunkerM vs HiveMQ vs EMQX: open-source MQTT management compared"

docs.title: Type 3 — The AI Differentiation Angle
docs.text:
This is your most unique positioning. Nobody else has plain-English device control over MQTT at this price point. - "Controlling industrial equipment with plain English over MQTT — no payload knowledge required" - "How AI-assisted MQTT management reduces configuration errors in production environments"

Publish one article per week on the blog at bunkerai.dev and cross-post to LinkedIn and Reddit (r/homeautomation, r/selfhosted, r/MQTT, r/iot).

docs.title: Phase 4 — System Integrators as a Force Multiplier (Month 2+)
docs.text: One system integrator can bring 10 enterprise clients. This channel is often ignored by early-stage software companies.

docs.title: How to Identify Them
docs.text: Search LinkedIn for "IoT system integrator [country]" or "industrial automation consultant". Look for companies with 5-50 employees that already work with MQTT-based systems.

docs.title: The Pitch to Integrators
docs.text:
• BunkerM is free to deploy for their clients (Community edition)
  • They can add a management layer on top of existing Mosquitto deployments
  • Enterprise plan gives their clients a supported, compliant product with SLA
  • Consider a reseller margin or referral fee on Enterprise deals they bring in

docs.title: Activation
docs.text: Reach out to 5-10 integrators per week with a personalized LinkedIn message (not a template blast). Reference a specific sector they work in. Offer a free 30-minute technical walkthrough.

docs.title: Budget Recommendation — Starting Small
docs.text:
Phase Channel Monthly Budget Expected Outcome Phase 0 Funnel fixes (Tally, one-pager) €0 Better conversion on existing traffic Phase 1 LinkedIn Lead Gen (test) €200 one-time 5-15 qualified leads to evaluate Phase 2a Facebook Pixel + retargeting €50/mo Top-of-mind for warm visitors Phase 2b Facebook cold (Community growth) €50/mo More Docker pulls and GitHub stars Phase 3 Blog content €0 (time only) Compounding inbound over 3-6 months Phase 4 Integrator outreach €0 Highest ROI channel if it lands

Total to start: €300 one-time + €100/mo ongoing

This is genuinely minimal. The goal of the first spend is not revenue — it is learning. Which job titles click? Which sectors respond? Which ad creative gets leads? Spend €300, get data, then scale what works.

docs.title: Tracking and Success Metrics
docs.text:
Set these up before spending a single euro on ads:

Metric Tool Target Website visitors by source Google Analytics 4 (free) Baseline first 2 weeks, then compare Leads from LinkedIn LinkedIn Campaign Manager Cost per lead under €30 Demo bookings Calendly (free tier) 2-5 per month to start Community to Enterprise conversion Manual tracking in a spreadsheet 1 enterprise inquiry per 200 Community installs GitHub stars growth rate GitHub Insights Maintain 30+ new stars per month Docker pulls growth Docker Hub Maintain 10-20% month-over-month growth

docs.title: 90-Day Action Plan
docs.text: Week Action Cost 1 Add production user survey to dashboard €0 1 Write Enterprise one-pager (PDF + website section) €0 1 Post GitHub Discussions activation thread €0 1 Install Facebook Pixel on bunkerai.dev €0 2 Launch LinkedIn lead gen campaign (Germany or France, manufacturing) €200 2 Post LinkedIn milestone content (440 stars, AI demo video) €0 3 Activate Facebook retargeting for /pricing visitors €50 3 Publish first sector-specific blog article (manufacturing) €0 4 Review LinkedIn results, adjust targeting or creative €0 4 Begin integrator outreach (5 per week on LinkedIn) €0 5-8 Publish one blog article per week €0 5-8 Respond to every inbound with a demo offer within 24h €0 8 Full review: what is the cost per lead, which sector converted, where to double down €0

docs.title: Key Principles to Keep in Mind
docs.text:
• Fix the funnel before filling it. Ads on a leaky funnel waste money. The one-pager and demo booking flow must exist before you run a single ad.
  • Enterprise sales are slow. A deal that starts with a LinkedIn ad in week 2 may close in month 4. Consistency matters more than bursts.
  • The Community edition is your best sales rep. Every person who installs BunkerM is a potential enterprise buyer or a referral source. Keep improving it.
  • Your AI features are your moat. No other self-hosted MQTT manager has plain-English device control. Lead with this in every ad, post, and pitch.
  • Respond fast to every inbound. Speed of response is the single highest-ROI action in early enterprise sales. A reply within 2 hours beats a polished pitch 3 days later.

docs.title: What is BunkerM?
docs.text: BunkerM is an all-in-one Mosquitto MQTT broker with a comprehensive web UI for easy management. It combines a pre-configured Mosquitto broker with a powerful management interface, dynamic security controls, monitoring capabilities, and cloud integration features.

docs.title: Is BunkerM open source?
docs.text: Yes, BunkerM Community Edition is open source and freely available. There are also Pro and Enterprise editions with additional features for commercial use.

docs.title: What platforms does BunkerM support?
docs.text: BunkerM runs on any platform that supports Docker, including: - Linux (all major distributions) - Windows - macOS - Raspberry Pi and other ARM-based devices

docs.title: What MQTT versions does BunkerM support?
docs.text: BunkerM supports: - MQTT 3.1 - MQTT 3.1.1 - MQTT 5.0

docs.title: What are the system requirements for BunkerM?
docs.text: Minimum requirements: - Docker 19.03 or higher - 1+ CPU cores - 512MB RAM (1GB+ recommended) - 1GB free disk space - Ports 1900 and 2000 available

docs.title: How do I install BunkerM?
docs.text:
The simplest way is using Docker:

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm

For persistent data:

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 \
  -v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto \
  -v mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto \
  -v auth_data:/data \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm

docs.title: What ports does BunkerM use?
docs.text:
By default, BunkerM uses: - Port 1900 for MQTT communication - Port 2000 for the web interface

These can be mapped to different ports if needed.

docs.title: How do I access the web interface?
docs.text: Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost:2000 (or the appropriate address if installed on a remote server).

docs.title: What are the default login credentials?
docs.text:
The default credentials are: - Username: admin@example.com - Password: password123

You should change these immediately after your first login.

docs.title: How many clients can connect to BunkerM?
docs.text: BunkerM can handle thousands of concurrent client connections, limited only by your system resources. The default configuration allows unlimited connections, but you can set a limit in the broker configuration.

docs.title: Does BunkerM support WebSockets?
docs.text: Yes, BunkerM supports MQTT over WebSockets, allowing web applications to connect directly to the broker.

docs.title: Does BunkerM support TLS/SSL?
docs.text: Yes, BunkerM can be configured to use TLS/SSL for secure communications. The default configuration uses HTTP for simplicity, but you can configure it to use your own certificates for HTTPS.

docs.title: Can I use BunkerM with existing MQTT clients?
docs.text: Yes, BunkerM is compatible with any standard MQTT client, including: - Mosquitto clients - MQTT.js - Paho - HiveMQ clients - And many others

docs.title: How does BunkerM handle authentication?
docs.text: BunkerM uses username and password authentication for MQTT clients. You can create and manage client credentials through the web interface.

docs.title: How does access control work in BunkerM?
docs.text: BunkerM uses a role-based access control system: 1. Create roles with specific topic permissions 2. Assign roles to clients directly or via groups 3. Clients can only access topics according to their roles' permissions

docs.title: Is communication between clients and the broker encrypted?
docs.text: Yes, BunkerM supports TLS/SSL encryption for secure communication between clients and the broker.

docs.title: Can I integrate BunkerM with external authentication systems?
docs.text: The Enterprise Edition supports integration with LDAP and OAuth 2.0/JWT authentication systems.

docs.title: How can I monitor broker performance?
docs.text: BunkerM provides a comprehensive dashboard showing: - Connected clients - Message throughput - Subscription counts - Retained message counts - Real-time activity logs

docs.title: Can I export logs and metrics?
docs.text: Yes, you can export broker logs and performance metrics for external analysis or record-keeping.

docs.title: How do I back up my BunkerM configuration?
docs.text:
To back up your configuration and data:

docker run --rm -v mosquitto_data:/data -v $(pwd)/backup:/backup \
  alpine sh -c "cd /data && tar czf /backup/mosquitto_data.tar.gz ."

docs.title: Can I manage multiple brokers from one interface?
docs.text: The Enterprise Edition supports managing multiple brokers from a single interface.

docs.title: What cloud platforms does BunkerM integrate with?
docs.text: The Pro and Enterprise editions support integration with: - AWS IoT Core - Azure IoT Hub

docs.title: How does the cloud integration work?
docs.text: BunkerM creates a bridge between your local MQTT broker and the cloud platform, allowing bidirectional message flow between local devices and cloud services.

docs.title: Do I need cloud integration to use BunkerM?
docs.text: No, cloud integration is an optional feature. BunkerM functions perfectly as a standalone MQTT broker without any cloud connectivity.

docs.title: Why can't my clients connect to the broker?
docs.text:
Common reasons include: - Incorrect client credentials - Firewall blocking port 1900 - Broker not running - Reaching maximum connection limit

Check the Troubleshooting Guide for detailed solutions.

docs.title: How do I reset the admin password if I forget it?
docs.text:
If you've forgotten the admin password, you can reset it by:

docker exec -it <container_id> /bin/bash
cd /app
node reset-admin-password.js

This will reset the password to the default "admin".

docs.title: What are Agents?
docs.text:
Agents are local automations that run directly on your BunkerM instance — no cloud connection required to execute them:

  • Schedulers publish an MQTT message on a cron schedule (e.g. "turn on pump every day at 6am").
  • Watchers monitor an MQTT topic and fire a notification when a condition is met (e.g. "alert me when temperature exceeds 30°C").

docs.title: How many agents can I create on the Community edition?
docs.text: Up to 2 agents combined (schedulers + watchers). This is enforced both in the UI and server-side.

docs.title: Do agents require BunkerAI Cloud to run?
docs.text: No. Agents execute entirely on your local infrastructure. The MQTT broker, watcher engine, and scheduler all run inside the BunkerM Docker container. BunkerAI Cloud is not involved in agent execution.

docs.title: What happens if I run out of credits while having agents running?
docs.text: Nothing happens to your agents. Credits fund AI features (chat assistant, Telegram/Slack notifications, anomaly alerts). When credits are exhausted, AI features pause. Local agents — schedulers and watchers — keep running indefinitely.

docs.title: What happens to my agents if I go offline or cancel my Premium subscription?
docs.text: Agents you have already created continue running on your local infrastructure. The 2-agent limit for Community applies at creation time, not at execution time. Agents are yours to keep permanently.

docs.title: Do I need internet to use agents?
docs.text: Only for the initial one-time activation (free). After that, agents work completely offline. For air-gapped deployments, see the activation question below.

docs.title: Why does BunkerM require activation?
docs.text: A one-time free activation lets us enforce the 2-agent Community limit server-side (in addition to the frontend), which prevents the limit from being bypassed by calling the API directly. The activation is cryptographically signed and verified locally — no ongoing cloud dependency after the first activation.

docs.title: How does activation work?
docs.text:
1. On first start, BunkerM silently attempts auto-activation by contacting BunkerAI Cloud.
  2. If successful, a signed license key is stored locally. Done — you never need to think about it again.
  3. If BunkerAI Cloud is unreachable (air-gapped network), a banner appears in the dashboard with your Instance ID.

docs.title: How do I activate BunkerM in an air-gapped environment?
docs.text:
1. Note your Instance ID shown in the activation banner (e.g. BKMR-A1B2C3D4).
  2. From any internet-connected device, visit bunkerai.dev and create a free account.
  3. Enter your Instance ID to generate your Community key.
  4. Copy the key and paste it into the activation field in the BunkerM dashboard.
  5. Click Apply. BunkerM stores the key locally — no internet needed after this point.

docs.title: Does the activation key expire?
docs.text: No. Community license keys do not expire. Once activated, your instance remains activated permanently, even without any internet connection.

docs.title: Does my activation key work on multiple BunkerM instances?
docs.text: No. Each key is cryptographically bound to the Instance ID it was generated for. A separate (free) activation is required for each deployment. This prevents key sharing while keeping the process free.

docs.title: What is the Instance ID?
docs.text: A unique identifier generated on BunkerM's first start, stored in the persistent data volume. It survives container rebuilds on the same host (docker compose down/up). A fresh volume on a new host generates a new Instance ID requiring a new (free) activation.

docs.title: What's the difference between Community and Premium?
docs.text:
Community Premium Price Free forever Credits-based Agents Up to 2 Unlimited Agent execution Local, no cloud Local, no cloud AI chat assistant ✗ ✓ (credits) Telegram / Slack alerts ✗ ✓ (credits) Activation One-time, free One-time

Key principle: Agents are yours to keep once created — on any tier. Credits fund ongoing AI features, not agent execution.

docs.title: How does the credit system work?
docs.text:
Credits are consumed by AI-powered features: natural language chat, anomaly alert forwarding to Telegram/Slack, and AI-generated watchers/schedulers. Local agent execution (the schedulers and watchers firing on your broker) consumes zero credits.

When your credit balance reaches zero, AI features pause until you top up. Your agents, ACL configuration, and monitoring continue working normally.

docs.title: How do I get support for BunkerM?
docs.text:
• Community Edition: GitHub issues and community forums
  • Premium: Email support at support@bunkerai.dev, priority issue resolution

docs.title: How can I contribute to BunkerM?
docs.text: You can contribute to BunkerM by: - Submitting pull requests on GitHub - Reporting bugs and suggesting features - Improving documentation - Sharing your experience with the community

docs.title: How do I upgrade from Community to Premium?
docs.text: Visit bunkerai.dev to create an account and purchase a credit bundle. Premium unlocks unlimited agents and AI features.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: This guide provides solutions for common issues you might encounter when using BunkerM. If you're experiencing problems, follow the relevant troubleshooting steps below.

docs.title: Can't Access the Web UI
docs.text:
If you can't access the BunkerM web interface:

  1. Verify the container is running: 

    docker ps | grep bunkerm
    If the container isn't listed, restart it: 
    docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm

  2. Check port availability: Ensure port 2000 isn't being used by another application: 

    netstat -tuln | grep 2000

  3. Verify network connectivity: If accessing from another machine, ensure there are no firewall rules blocking port 2000.

  4. Check browser compatibility:

  5. Make sure you're using a modern, up-to-date browser
  6. Clear your browser cache if you're experiencing UI issues

docs.title: MQTT Clients Can't Connect
docs.text:
If MQTT clients can't connect to the broker:

  1. Verify the broker is running: Check the broker status in the dashboard.

  2. Check client credentials:

  3. Ensure the client username and password are correct

  4. Verify the client exists in the Client Management page

  5. Check port availability: Ensure port 1900 isn't being used by another application: 

    netstat -tuln | grep 1900

  6. Test with a simple client: 

    mosquitto_pub -h localhost -p 1900 -u your_client -P your_password -t test -m "test"

  7. Check broker logs: Review the Broker Logs for authentication failures or other errors.

docs.title: Slow Web UI
docs.text:
If the web interface is responding slowly:

  1. Check system resources: Verify your host has sufficient CPU and memory available.

  2. Reduce log verbosity: Lower the logging level in the broker configuration.

  3. Clear browser cache: Clear your browser's cache and cookies.

  4. Check network latency: If accessing remotely, network latency might be affecting performance.

docs.title: Broker Performance Problems
docs.text:
If the MQTT broker is experiencing performance issues:

  1. Check system resources: Monitor CPU, memory, and disk usage on the host system.

  2. Review connection count: Check how many clients are connected and whether you're approaching limits.

  3. Examine message rates: Look for unusually high message rates that might be overloading the broker.

  4. Check retained messages: Too many retained messages can impact performance.

  5. Review QoS settings: Higher QoS levels require more resources.

docs.title: Authentication Failures
docs.text:
If clients are failing to authenticate:

  1. Verify client credentials: Double-check the username and password in the Client Management page.

  2. Check for special characters: Ensure passwords don't contain special characters that might need escaping.

  3. Review broker logs: Check the logs for specific authentication error messages.

docs.title: Permission Denied Errors
docs.text:
If clients can connect but can't publish or subscribe:

  1. Check ACL rules: Review the role permissions assigned to the client.

  2. Verify topic syntax: Ensure the topic matches the pattern specified in the ACL rules.

  3. Check group assignments: Verify the client is in the correct groups with the necessary roles.

  4. Test with a simpler topic: Try publishing to a simple topic to isolate the issue.

docs.title: Lost Configuration After Restart
docs.text:
If your configuration changes don't persist after container restart:

  1. Use volume mounts: Ensure you're using volume mounts for persistent data:

bash docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 \ -v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto \ -v mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto \ -v auth_data:/data \ bunkeriot/bunkerm

  1. Check volume permissions: Ensure the container has write permissions to the mounted volumes.

  2. Verify changes were saved: Some changes require explicit saving through the UI.

docs.title: Lost Messages
docs.text:
If retained messages are being lost:

  1. Check persistence settings: Verify that persistence is enabled in the broker configuration.

  2. Check storage location: Ensure the persistence directory is on a mounted volume.

  3. Verify disk space: Check that there's sufficient disk space available.

docs.title: Failed Updates
docs.text:
If you encounter issues when updating BunkerM:

  1. Check compatibility: Verify that the new version is compatible with your setup.

  2. Backup before updating: Always backup your data before updating: bash docker run --rm -v mosquitto_data:/data -v $(pwd)/backup:/backup \ alpine sh -c "cd /data && tar czf /backup/mosquitto_data.tar.gz ."

  3. Check logs after update: Review the logs for any errors after updating.

docs.title: Migration Problems
docs.text:
If you're having trouble migrating from one version to another:

  1. Follow the migration guide: Check the release notes for specific migration instructions.

  2. Perform a clean install if necessary: Sometimes a clean install with data import is easier than migration.

docs.title: Container Won't Start
docs.text:
If the Docker container won't start:

  1. Check Docker logs: bash docker logs <container_id>

  2. Verify Docker version: Ensure you're running a supported Docker version.

  3. Check resource constraints: Verify that Docker has sufficient resources allocated.

  4. Try with a clean container: Remove the container and create a new one: bash docker rm <container_id> docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm

docs.title: Volume Mount Issues
docs.text:
If you're having problems with volume mounts:

  1. Check volume existence: bash docker volume ls | grep mosquitto

  2. Inspect volume details: bash docker volume inspect mosquitto_data

  3. Check permissions: Ensure the Docker user has permissions to access the volume locations.

docs.title: Getting Additional Help
docs.text:
If you're still experiencing issues after trying these troubleshooting steps:

  1. Check the documentation: Review the relevant sections of this documentation for more detailed guidance.

  2. Search for similar issues: Check if others have reported similar problems in the GitHub repository.

  3. Contact support: Reach out to the BunkerM team at m.idrissi@bunkeriot.com.

  4. Community forums: Post your question on community forums or discussion groups.

  5. Verify that port 2000 is available and not blocked by a firewall

  6. Ensure Docker is running properly
  7. Check that the container is running with docker ps

docs.title: Dynamic Security
docs.text: BunkerM leverages Mosquitto's Dynamic Security Plugin to provide flexible, runtime-configurable access control for your MQTT broker. This powerful feature allows you to manage client authentication and authorization without restarting the broker.

docs.title: Understanding Dynamic Security
docs.text:
The Dynamic Security Plugin is an extension for Mosquitto that replaces the traditional static password and ACL file approach with a dynamic, JSON-based configuration system. BunkerM provides a user-friendly interface to manage this system.

Key benefits include:

  • Runtime Configuration: Change security settings without broker restarts
  • Fine-grained Access Control: Detailed control over who can access what
  • Hierarchical Structure: Organize clients into groups with inherited permissions
  • Flexible Role System: Define reusable permission sets
  • Import/Export Capability: Easily backup or transfer configurations

docs.title: Dynamic Security Components
docs.text: The dynamic security system in BunkerM consists of several key components:

docs.title: Clients
docs.text: Clients represent MQTT client connections and contain: - Username and password for authentication - Optional group memberships - Optional direct role assignments

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: Roles define sets of permissions that can be assigned to clients or groups: - Topic access rules (publish/subscribe permissions) - Access control lists (ACLs) - Priority levels for conflict resolution

docs.title: Groups
docs.text: Groups organize clients and provide: - Collective role assignments - Hierarchical structure (groups can contain other groups) - Simplified management for multiple clients

docs.title: ACLs
docs.text: Access Control Lists define: - Which topics a client can publish to - Which topics a client can subscribe to - Whether access is allowed or denied

docs.title: Import/Export ACL
docs.text: BunkerM provides a dedicated interface for importing and exporting the dynamic security configuration:

docs.title: Exporting Configuration
docs.text:
To export your current dynamic security configuration:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Import/export ACL
  2. In the "Export ACL" section, click Export ACL
  3. The system will generate a JSON file containing all clients, roles, and groups
  4. Save the file to your local system

This exported file can be used for: - Backup purposes - Transferring configuration between environments - Version control of your security settings

docs.title: Importing Configuration
docs.text:
To import a dynamic security configuration:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Import/export ACL
  2. In the "Import ACL" section, click the file input field
  3. Select a valid dynamic security JSON file
  4. Click Import ACL

The import process will add all clients, roles, and groups defined in the JSON file to your BunkerM instance.

Warning

Importing a configuration will not remove existing entries. If you want to completely replace your configuration, consider resetting it first.

docs.title: Dynamic Security JSON Format
docs.text:
The dynamic security configuration uses a specific JSON format defined by the Mosquitto project. Here's a simplified example:

{
  "clients": [
    {
      "username": "client1",
      "password": "hashedpassword",
      "clientid": "client_1",
      "groups": ["group1"],
      "roles": ["role1"]
    }
  ],
  "groups": [
    {
      "groupname": "group1",
      "roles": ["role2"]
    }
  ],
  "roles": [
    {
      "rolename": "role1",
      "acls": [
        {
          "acltype": "publishClientSend",
          "topic": "sensors/#",
          "allow": true
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "rolename": "role2",
      "acls": [
        {
          "acltype": "subscribePattern",
          "topic": "commands/#",
          "allow": true
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}

For the complete specification, refer to the official Mosquitto documentation.

docs.title: Permission Evaluation
docs.text:
When a client attempts to publish to or subscribe to a topic, the dynamic security plugin evaluates permissions in the following order:

  1. Direct Client ACLs: ACLs directly assigned to the client via roles
  2. Group ACLs: ACLs inherited from groups the client belongs to
  3. Default Access: If no matching ACL is found, access is denied

When multiple ACLs match a topic:

  1. More specific topic patterns take precedence over more general ones
  2. Deny rules take precedence over allow rules
  3. Higher priority roles take precedence over lower priority roles

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Follow the principle of least privilege
  • Use specific topic patterns rather than broad wildcards
  • Regularly audit your security configuration
  • Back up your dynamic security configuration regularly

docs.title: Organization
docs.text:
• Create a logical group hierarchy
  • Use descriptive names for roles and groups
  • Document your security model
  • Use roles for functional permissions, not individual clients

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Keep your security configuration as simple as possible
  • Avoid excessive nesting of groups
  • Use specific topic patterns to improve ACL evaluation performance

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Client Management - Managing MQTT client accounts
  • Role Management - Configuring access control roles
  • Group Management - Organizing clients into groups
  • Password Management - Managing client credentials

docs.title: Password Management
docs.text: BunkerM provides advanced password management features to help you migrate from existing Mosquitto installations and manage client credentials efficiently.

docs.title: Importing from Mosquitto Password File
docs.text: If you're migrating from a standard Mosquitto broker, BunkerM allows you to import users from a mosquitto_passwd file while preserving their original password hashes.

docs.title: What is a Mosquitto Password File?
docs.text:
A Mosquitto password file is created and managed using the mosquitto_passwd utility that comes with Mosquitto. It contains usernames and hashed passwords for MQTT clients.

Example of a mosquitto_passwd file content (not human-readable): 

user1:$6$QrAVXYZ$kbGxQa5jgDfIYE/...
user2:$6$AbCdEfG$hJkLmNoP/qRsTuV...

docs.title: Import Process
docs.text:
To import users from a Mosquitto password file:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Import Mosquitto Password File
  2. Click the file input field and select your mosquitto_passwd file
  3. Click Import Users
  4. The system will process the file and display import results

docs.title: Import Results
docs.text:
After the import process completes, BunkerM will display a summary of the results:

  • Total Users: The total number of users found in the file
  • Successfully Imported: Users that were successfully added to BunkerM
  • Skipped: Users that already exist in BunkerM (based on username)
  • Failed: Users that could not be imported due to errors

For each user, you'll see detailed information about the import status and any issues encountered.

docs.title: Considerations
docs.text:
When importing users from a Mosquitto password file:

  • Usernames must be unique; if a username already exists in BunkerM, the import for that user will be skipped
  • The password hashes are preserved, allowing users to continue using their existing passwords
  • Imported users will not have any roles or group memberships assigned by default
  • After import, you'll need to assign appropriate roles and groups to the imported users

docs.title: Password Security
docs.text: BunkerM implements strong password security practices:

docs.title: Password Hashing
docs.text: All passwords in BunkerM are securely hashed using modern cryptographic algorithms. The system never stores passwords in plain text.

docs.title: Password Requirements
docs.text:
When creating or updating passwords, BunkerM enforces the following requirements:

  • Minimum length: 8 characters
  • Should contain a mix of uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters

docs.title: Password Rotation
docs.text:
For enhanced security, consider implementing a password rotation policy:

  1. Regularly update client passwords
  2. Use unique passwords for each client
  3. Keep a record of password changes

docs.title: Resetting Admin Password
docs.text:
If you've forgotten the admin password for the BunkerM web interface:

  1. Access the Docker container: 

    docker exec -it <container_id> /bin/bash

  2. Navigate to the application directory: 

    cd /app

  3. Run the password reset script: 

    node reset-admin-password.js

  4. The admin password will be reset to the default: admin

  5. Log in with the default password and immediately change it to a secure password

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Use strong, unique passwords for each client
  • Regularly rotate passwords, especially for critical clients
  • Limit access to password files and backups
  • Use TLS/SSL encryption to protect password transmission

docs.title: Migration
docs.text:
• Back up your existing configuration before importing
  • Test the import process in a non-production environment first
  • Verify client connectivity after migration
  • Document the migration process and results

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
• Establish a clear password policy
  • Document password reset procedures
  • Consider using a password manager for administrative credentials
  • Audit password changes regularly

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Client Management - Managing MQTT client accounts
  • Dynamic Security - Advanced security features
  • Broker Configuration - Configuring authentication settings
  • Troubleshooting - Solving authentication issues

docs.title: AWS IoT Core Integration
docs.text:
BunkerM Pro offers seamless integration with AWS IoT Core, allowing you to bridge your local MQTT broker with AWS's cloud-based IoT services. This integration enables bidirectional communication between your local devices and AWS IoT applications.

Pro Feature

AWS IoT Core integration is available in the Pro and Enterprise editions of BunkerM.

docs.title: Benefits of AWS IoT Core Integration
docs.text:
Connecting BunkerM to AWS IoT Core provides several advantages:

  • Edge-to-Cloud Connectivity: Connect local devices to AWS cloud services
  • Data Processing: Leverage AWS services for data analytics and storage
  • Serverless Applications: Trigger AWS Lambda functions from MQTT messages
  • Device Shadows: Use AWS IoT Device Shadows for offline device state management
  • Security: Utilize AWS's robust security features alongside BunkerM's local security

docs.title: Configuration Overview
docs.text: The AWS IoT Bridge configuration page allows you to set up and manage the connection between BunkerM and AWS IoT Core.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before configuring the AWS IoT Bridge, you'll need:

  1. An active AWS account
  2. An AWS IoT Core endpoint
  3. X.509 certificates for authentication:
  4. Client certificate
  5. Private key
  6. Root CA certificate

docs.title: Setting Up AWS IoT Core
docs.text:
1. In the AWS Management Console, navigate to IoT Core
  2. Create a new Thing or use an existing one
  3. Create and download certificates
  4. Create a policy that allows connecting, publishing, and subscribing
  5. Attach the policy to your certificates

docs.title: Configuring the Bridge
docs.text:
To set up the AWS IoT Bridge in BunkerM:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Integration > AWS IoT Bridge in the left sidebar
  2. Enter the following information:
  3. AWS IoT Endpoint: Your AWS IoT endpoint (e.g., xxxxxxx-ats.iot.region.amazonaws.com)
  4. Client ID: A unique identifier for the bridge connection

  5. Topics: The MQTT topics to bridge (one per line)

  6. Upload the required certificate files:

  7. Certificate File (.pem): The client certificate from AWS
  8. Private Key File (.pem): The private key from AWS

  9. CA Certificate File (.pem): The root CA certificate

  10. Click Configure Bridge

docs.title: Topic Mapping
docs.text: The AWS IoT Bridge supports bidirectional topic mapping between your local MQTT broker and AWS IoT Core:

docs.title: Local to AWS Direction
docs.text:
Messages published to specified topics on your local broker will be forwarded to AWS IoT Core with the same topic name.

Example: - Local topic: sensors/temperature - AWS IoT Core receives: sensors/temperature

docs.title: AWS to Local Direction
docs.text:
Messages published to specified topics on AWS IoT Core will be forwarded to your local broker with the same topic name.

Example: - AWS IoT Core topic: actuators/switch - Local broker receives: actuators/switch

docs.title: Advanced Configuration
docs.text: For more complex setups, you can configure advanced options:

docs.title: Topic Transformations
docs.text:
You can transform topic names during bridging:

  • Prefix Addition: Add a prefix to topics when forwarding
  • Topic Remapping: Change the topic structure completely

docs.title: QoS Settings
docs.text:
Configure the Quality of Service level for bridged messages:

  • QoS 0: At most once delivery
  • QoS 1: At least once delivery
  • QoS 2: Exactly once delivery

docs.title: Connection Settings
docs.text:
Fine-tune the connection parameters:

  • Keep Alive Interval: How often to send keep-alive messages
  • Reconnect Delay: Time to wait before reconnecting after a failure
  • Connection Timeout: How long to wait for a connection to establish

docs.title: Monitoring the Bridge
docs.text:
BunkerM provides tools to monitor the health and activity of your AWS IoT Bridge:

  1. Connection Status: View the current connection state
  2. Message Statistics: Track messages flowing in both directions
  3. Error Logs: Identify and troubleshoot connection issues

docs.title: Security Considerations
docs.text:
When using the AWS IoT Bridge, keep these security best practices in mind:

  • Store certificates securely
  • Use specific topic filters rather than wildcards when possible
  • Regularly rotate certificates according to your security policy
  • Monitor bridge activity for unusual patterns

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: If you encounter issues with the AWS IoT Bridge:

docs.title: Connection Problems
docs.text:
• Verify your AWS IoT endpoint is correct
  • Check that your certificates are valid and properly formatted
  • Ensure your AWS IoT policy allows the necessary actions

docs.title: Message Flow Issues
docs.text:
• Confirm that topics are correctly specified in the bridge configuration
  • Check that local clients have permission to access bridged topics
  • Verify that AWS IoT Core is receiving and processing messages

docs.title: Related Documentation
docs.text:
• Azure IoT Hub - Similar integration with Microsoft's IoT platform
  • Broker Configuration - Configure your local broker settings
  • ACL Configuration - Set up access control for bridged topics

docs.title: First Login
docs.text: This guide will walk you through the initial login process and the essential first steps to secure and configure your BunkerM installation.

docs.title: Accessing the Login Page
docs.text:
After installing BunkerM, you can access the web interface:

  1. Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost:2000 (or the appropriate address if installed on a remote server)
  2. You should now see the BunkerM login page

docs.title: Default Credentials
docs.text:
For your first login, use the following default credentials:

  • Username: admin@example.com
  • Password: password123

docs.title: Changing the Default Password
docs.text:
For security reasons, you should change the default password immediately after your first login:

  1. After logging in, click on your username in the top-right corner
  2. Select Account Settings from the dropdown menu
  3. In the Change Password section, enter your current password and your new password twice
  4. Click Update Password

Warning

Using the default credentials in a production environment poses a significant security risk. Always change the default password immediately.

docs.title: Initial Dashboard Overview
docs.text:
After logging in, you'll be taken to the main dashboard, which provides an overview of your MQTT broker's status:

  • Connected Clients: Shows the number of currently connected MQTT clients
  • Message Statistics: Displays message throughput metrics
  • Broker Status: Indicates whether the broker is running properly
  • Recent Activity: Shows recent connection and message events

docs.title: 1. Configure Broker Settings
docs.text:
Before using BunkerM in production, you should review and adjust the broker configuration:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Broker Configuration in the left sidebar
  2. Review the default settings and adjust them according to your needs
  3. Pay special attention to:
  4. Maximum connections
  5. Maximum message size
  6. Persistence settings
  7. Logging levels
  8. Click Save Configuration when done

docs.title: 2. Create Administrator Accounts
docs.text:
If multiple people will be managing the broker, create separate administrator accounts:

  1. Navigate to Administration > User Management
  2. Click Add User
  3. Fill in the user details and select the appropriate role
  4. Click Save

docs.title: 3. Set Up Your First MQTT Client
docs.text:
To test that everything is working correctly:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Client Management
  2. Click Add Client
  3. Enter a username and password for your test client
  4. Click Save

docs.title: 4. Configure Access Control
docs.text:
BunkerM uses a role-based access control system for MQTT clients:

  1. Navigate to MQTT Management > Role Management
  2. Click Add Role
  3. Create a basic role with appropriate permissions
  4. Assign this role to your test client

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After completing the initial setup, you can:

  • Configure more detailed ACL rules
  • Set up client groups
  • Explore monitoring capabilities
  • Configure cloud integrations (Pro feature)

For more detailed information on any of these topics, refer to the specific sections in this documentation.

docs.title: Installation Guide
docs.text: This guide covers the different ways to install and deploy BunkerM, the all-in-one Mosquitto MQTT broker with web UI.

docs.title: System Requirements
docs.text:
Before deploying BunkerM, ensure your system meets the following requirements:

  • Docker: Version 19.03 or higher
  • CPU: 1+ cores
  • RAM: 512MB minimum (1GB+ recommended)
  • Storage: 1GB minimum free space
  • Ports: 1900 (MQTT) and 2000 (Web UI) must be available

docs.title: Docker Installation
docs.text: The recommended way to install BunkerM is using Docker. This method provides the simplest setup experience and works across all major platforms.

docs.title: Basic Deployment
docs.text:
docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm

This command: - Runs BunkerM in detached mode (-d) - Maps the MQTT broker port to 1900 (-p 1900:1900) - Maps the Web UI port to 2000 (-p 2000:2000) - Uses the official BunkerM image (bunkeriot/bunkerm)

docs.title: Persistent Data Deployment
docs.text:
For production environments, you'll want to persist your data across container restarts:

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 \
  -v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto \
  -v mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto \
  -v auth_data:/data \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm

This command adds: - A volume for Mosquitto data (-v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto) - A volume for authentication data (-v auth_data:/data)

docs.title: Remote Access Installation
docs.text:
If you need to access BunkerM from outside the host machine:

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 \
  -e HOST_ADDRESS=<HOST_IP> \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm

Replace <HOST_IP> with your host machine's IP address or domain name.

docs.title: Docker Compose Installation
docs.text:
For more complex setups, you can use Docker Compose:

  1. Create a file named docker-compose.yml with the following content:
version: '3'
services:
  bunkerm:
    image: bunkeriot/bunkerm
    ports:
      - "1900:1900"
      - "2000:2000"
    volumes:
      - mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto
      - auth_data:/data
    environment:
      - HOST_ADDRESS=localhost  # Change this for remote access
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  mosquitto_data:
  auth_data:
  1. Run the following command in the same directory:
docker-compose up -d

docs.title: Kubernetes Installation
docs.text:
For Kubernetes deployments:

  1. Create a file named bunkerm-deployment.yaml with the following content:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: bunkerm
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: bunkerm
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: bunkerm
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: bunkerm
        image: bunkeriot/bunkerm
        ports:
        - containerPort: 1900
          name: mqtt
        - containerPort: 2000
          name: webui
        volumeMounts:
        - name: mosquitto-data
          mountPath: /var/lib/mosquitto
        - name: auth-data
          mountPath: /data
      volumes:
      - name: mosquitto-data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: mosquitto-data-pvc
      - name: auth-data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: auth-data-pvc
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: bunkerm
spec:
  selector:
    app: bunkerm
  ports:
  - name: mqtt
    port: 1900
    targetPort: 1900
  - name: webui
    port: 2000
    targetPort: 2000
  type: LoadBalancer
  1. Apply the configuration:
kubectl apply -f bunkerm-deployment.yaml

docs.title: Verifying the Installation
docs.text:
After installation, verify that BunkerM is running correctly:

  1. Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost:2000
  2. You should see the BunkerM login page

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
If you encounter issues during installation:

  • Web UI not accessible: Verify that port 2000 is not being used by another application
  • MQTT broker not accessible: Verify that port 1900 is not being used by another application
  • Container fails to start: Check Docker logs with docker logs <container_id>

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text: Now that you have BunkerM installed, proceed to the Quick Start Guide to learn how to configure and use your MQTT broker.

docs.title: Running BunkerM with Persistent Storage
docs.text:
By default, if you run BunkerM without any volumes, all data is stored inside the container's writable layer. When you stop and remove the container, everything is lost - your MQTT clients, ACL rules, broker configuration, web UI accounts, and agent data all disappear.

Persistent storage keeps your data intact across container restarts, upgrades, and recreation.

docs.title: Why Persistence Matters
docs.text:
BunkerM stores several categories of data that you almost always want to keep:

  • MQTT clients and ACLs - every client account, role, and group you have created
  • Broker configuration - your Mosquitto settings, port configuration, and options
  • Dynamic security - the full Mosquitto dynamic security JSON file
  • Web UI users - BunkerM admin and user accounts
  • BunkerAI settings - API keys, integration tokens, and configuration
  • Agents - Watchers and Scheduled Jobs you have defined
  • Anomaly history - detected anomalies and their status
  • Topic annotations - AI context hints for your topics
  • Client logs - connection event history

Without volumes, all of this is recreated from scratch every time the container starts.

docs.title: What Data BunkerM Stores and Where
docs.text: Data Location inside container Volume to use Web UI users, BunkerAI settings, agents, anomaly data, annotations, client logs /nextjs/data bunkerm_data MQTT persistence (retained messages, QoS queues), dynamic security (clients/ACLs) /var/lib/mosquitto mosquitto_data Mosquitto configuration files /etc/mosquitto mosquitto_conf

docs.title: Volume Setup with docker run
docs.text:
Full command with all three persistent volumes:

docker run -d \
  -p 1900:1900 \
  -p 2000:2000 \
  -v bunkerm_data:/nextjs/data \
  -v mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto \
  -v mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto \
  --name bunkerm \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

Docker creates the named volumes automatically on first run. On subsequent starts, the same data is mounted back into the container.

docs.title: Volume Setup with Docker Compose
docs.text:
Create a docker-compose.yml file:

version: "3.8"
services:
  bunkerm:
    image: bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest
    ports:
      - "1900:1900"
      - "2000:2000"
    volumes:
      - bunkerm_data:/nextjs/data
      - mosquitto_data:/var/lib/mosquitto
      - mosquitto_conf:/etc/mosquitto
    restart: unless-stopped

volumes:
  bunkerm_data:
  mosquitto_data:
  mosquitto_conf:

Start:

docker compose up -d

Stop (data is preserved):

docker compose down

Stop and remove containers AND volumes (data is deleted):

docker compose down -v

Only use -v if you intentionally want to wipe all data and start fresh.

docs.title: Named Volumes vs Bind Mounts
docs.text: You can use either named volumes (as shown above) or bind mounts (host directory paths).

docs.title: Named volumes (recommended)
docs.text:
-v bunkerm_data:/nextjs/data

Docker manages the volume storage location. Data lives at /var/lib/docker/volumes/bunkerm_data/ on the host. Named volumes are portable, easy to back up, and work the same way on any Docker host.

docs.title: Bind mounts
docs.text:
-v /home/user/bunkerm/data:/nextjs/data

The container writes directly to a directory on your host filesystem. Useful if you want easy direct access to the files, or if you are integrating with other tools that read from specific paths.

Full docker run example with bind mounts:

mkdir -p /opt/bunkerm/data /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto/data /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto/conf

docker run -d \
  -p 1900:1900 \
  -p 2000:2000 \
  -v /opt/bunkerm/data:/nextjs/data \
  -v /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto/data:/var/lib/mosquitto \
  -v /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto/conf:/etc/mosquitto \
  --name bunkerm \
  --restart unless-stopped \
  bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

docs.title: Backing up named volumes
docs.text:
Use docker run with a temporary container to archive a volume's contents:

# Back up bunkerm_data
docker run --rm \
  -v bunkerm_data:/source:ro \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine tar czf /backup/bunkerm_data_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz -C /source .

# Back up mosquitto_data
docker run --rm \
  -v mosquitto_data:/source:ro \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine tar czf /backup/mosquitto_data_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz -C /source .

# Back up mosquitto_conf
docker run --rm \
  -v mosquitto_conf:/source:ro \
  -v $(pwd):/backup \
  alpine tar czf /backup/mosquitto_conf_$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz -C /source .

docs.title: Backing up bind mounts
docs.text:
Simply copy the directories:

cp -r /opt/bunkerm/data /opt/bunkerm/data.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d)
cp -r /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto /opt/bunkerm/mosquitto.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d)

docs.title: Automated daily backup
docs.text:
Add a cron job to run backups automatically:

0 2 * * * docker run --rm -v bunkerm_data:/source:ro -v /backups:/backup alpine tar czf /backup/bunkerm_$(date +\%Y\%m\%d).tar.gz -C /source . 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Upgrading BunkerM While Preserving Data
docs.text:
Because your data lives in volumes outside the container, upgrading is safe and straightforward:

  1. Pull the new image: 

    docker pull bunkeriot/bunkerm:latest

  2. Stop and remove the current container (data stays in volumes): 

    docker compose down
# or
docker stop bunkerm && docker rm bunkerm

  3. Start a new container with the same volumes (Docker Compose does this automatically): 

    docker compose up -d
# or
docker run -d ... (same command with same volume flags)

  4. Verify the new container started and your data is intact: 

    docker logs bunkerm
# Open http://localhost:2000 and confirm your clients and settings are present

The new container reads from the same volumes and picks up right where the old one left off. Your MQTT clients, ACL rules, web UI accounts, and all other data are preserved.

docs.title: Data Directory Overview
docs.text: Inside the container, here is what lives where:

docs.title: /nextjs/data
docs.text:
/nextjs/data/
  users.json                 - Web UI user accounts
  smart-anomaly.db           - Anomaly detection SQLite database (agents, anomalies, metrics)
  .bunkerai_status.json      - BunkerAI connection status

docs.title: /var/lib/mosquitto
docs.text:
/var/lib/mosquitto/
  dynamic-security.json      - All MQTT clients, roles, groups, and ACL rules
  mosquitto.db               - MQTT persistence (retained messages, QoS 1/2 queues)

docs.title: /etc/mosquitto
docs.text:
/etc/mosquitto/
  mosquitto.conf             - Main Mosquitto configuration
  conf.d/                    - Additional configuration snippets

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you get up and running with BunkerM quickly. We'll cover the basics of starting the container, accessing the web interface, and creating your first MQTT client.

docs.title: Starting BunkerM
docs.text:
The fastest way to start BunkerM is using Docker:

docker run -d -p 1900:1900 -p 2000:2000 bunkeriot/bunkerm

This command will: - Download the BunkerM image (if not already present) - Start a container in detached mode - Map port 1900 for MQTT communication - Map port 2000 for the web interface

docs.title: Accessing the Web Interface
docs.text:
1. Open your web browser and navigate to http://localhost:2000
  2. You should see the BunkerM login page

docs.title: First Login
docs.text:
For your first login, use the following credentials:

  • Username: admin
  • Password: admin

Warning

For security reasons, you should change the default password immediately after your first login.

docs.title: Creating Your First MQTT Client
docs.text:
Before you can connect to the MQTT broker, you need to create a client:

  1. In the left sidebar, navigate to MQTT Management > Client Management
  2. Click the Add client button in the top right corner
  3. Enter a username and password for your MQTT client
  4. Click Save

docs.title: Connecting to the MQTT Broker
docs.text:
Now that you've created a client, you can connect to the MQTT broker using any MQTT client application:

  • Broker Address: localhost (or your server's IP address)
  • Port: 1900
  • Username: The client username you created
  • Password: The client password you created

docs.title: Example with Mosquitto CLI
docs.text: mosquitto_pub -h localhost -p 1900 -u your_client_username -P your_client_password -t test/topic -m "Hello from BunkerM!"

docs.title: Example with MQTT Explorer
docs.text:
1. Download and install MQTT Explorer
  2. Create a new connection with the following settings:
  3. Host: localhost
  4. Port: 1900
  5. Username: Your client username
  6. Password: Your client password
  7. Click Connect

docs.title: Monitoring MQTT Activity
docs.text:
BunkerM provides real-time monitoring of MQTT activity:

  1. Navigate to the Dashboard in the left sidebar
  2. View the "Recent MQTT Events" panel to see real-time connection and message activity
  3. Check the "Broker Statistics" panel for overall performance metrics

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Now that you have BunkerM up and running, you can:

  • Configure broker settings
  • Set up role-based access control
  • Organize clients into groups
  • Monitor broker and client logs

For more detailed information, explore the rest of the documentation.

docs.title: Home Assistant Setup
docs.text: BunkerM is available as a native Home Assistant add-on. Running as an add-on means BunkerM is managed by Home Assistant's supervisor - it starts and stops with Home Assistant, logs are accessible from the HA UI, and all data is stored in HA's persistent data directory.

docs.title: What the HA Add-On Does
docs.text:
The BunkerM Home Assistant add-on runs the complete BunkerM stack - Mosquitto broker, web management dashboard, and all backend services - as a native HA add-on. You get the same full-featured BunkerM experience you would from a Docker deployment, but integrated into your Home Assistant environment.

This is the recommended way to run BunkerM if you are already on Home Assistant OS or Home Assistant Supervised.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Home Assistant OS or Home Assistant Supervised (Supervisor support required)
  • Internet access from the HA host (to download the add-on)
  • Access to the Home Assistant web interface

docs.title: Step 1: Add the BunkerM Add-On Repository
docs.text:
Home Assistant add-ons from third-party repositories need to be added manually:

  1. In Home Assistant, go to Settings > Add-ons.
  2. Click Add-on Store in the bottom-right corner.
  3. Click the three-dot menu (top-right) and select Repositories.
  4. In the "Manage add-on repositories" dialog, enter: 
    https://github.com/bunkeriot/bunkerm-ha-addons
  5. Click Add and then Close.

The BunkerM repository is now registered with your Home Assistant instance.

docs.title: Step 2: Install BunkerM
docs.text:
1. In the Add-on Store, scroll down or search for BunkerM.
  2. Click on the BunkerM add-on card.
  3. Click Install and wait for the download and installation to complete.

Installation may take a few minutes depending on your internet connection and hardware.

docs.title: Step 3: Configure the Add-On Options
docs.text:
Before starting the add-on, configure your credentials in the Configuration tab:

Option Default Description mqtt_username bunker Username for MQTT broker connections mqtt_password bunker Password for MQTT broker connections admin_email admin@bunker.local Login email for the BunkerM web UI admin_password admin123 Login password for the BunkerM web UI

Important: Change the default credentials before connecting any devices. The defaults are public and should not be used in production.

After setting your options, click Save.

docs.title: Step 4: Start the Add-On
docs.text:
1. Go to the Info tab of the BunkerM add-on.
  2. Click Start.
  3. Wait for the add-on to initialize (typically 10-20 seconds).
  4. Optionally enable Start on boot and Watchdog so the add-on automatically starts with Home Assistant and restarts if it crashes.

docs.title: Step 5: Open the BunkerM Web UI
docs.text:
Once the add-on is running, access the web interface at:

http://homeassistant.local:2000

Or substitute your Home Assistant's IP address:

http://192.168.1.x:2000

You can also click Open Web UI from the add-on info page in Home Assistant.

Log in with the admin_email and admin_password you configured in Step 3.

docs.title: Step 6: Connect Home Assistant's MQTT Integration to BunkerM
docs.text:
Now configure Home Assistant to use your BunkerM broker:

  1. In Home Assistant, go to Settings > Devices & Services.
  2. Click Add Integration and search for MQTT.
  3. If you already have an MQTT integration configured, click on it and update the broker settings.
  4. Enter the broker connection details:
  5. Broker: homeassistant.local or your HA IP address
  6. Port: 1883
  7. Username: the mqtt_username you set in the add-on options
  8. Password: the mqtt_password you set in the add-on options
  9. Click Submit and then Finish.

Home Assistant is now connected to your BunkerM MQTT broker.

docs.title: MQTT Port Note
docs.text: In the Home Assistant add-on, BunkerM uses the standard MQTT port 1883 (not 1900 as in the standalone Docker deployment). This is because most Home Assistant integrations and devices expect the standard MQTT port. Configure all your MQTT devices to connect on port 1883 when using the HA add-on.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text:
All BunkerM data is stored in Home Assistant's /data directory for the add-on. This means:

  • MQTT clients, ACLs, roles, and groups persist across add-on restarts
  • Web UI user accounts are preserved
  • Broker configuration is maintained
  • BunkerAI settings and agent data are stored persistently

Data is preserved when you restart or update the add-on. It is also included in Home Assistant backups if you use HA's snapshot/backup feature.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Add-on does not start: Check the add-on logs in the Log tab. Common issues include port conflicts if another service is already using port 2000 or 1883.

Cannot access the web UI: Verify the add-on is running (green status indicator). Check that port 2000 is not blocked by your network or HA host firewall.

HA MQTT integration cannot connect: Verify the username and password match what you set in the add-on options. Check the BunkerM ACL Clients page to confirm the broker user account exists and is enabled.

Add-on crashes repeatedly: Check the Log tab for error messages. If the issue persists, try stopping the add-on, waiting 30 seconds, and starting it again.

For additional help, open an issue on the BunkerM GitHub repository.

docs.title: Local LLM Integration (LM Studio)
docs.text: BunkerM can connect to a locally running large language model via LM Studio, giving you a fully private, offline-capable AI assistant that has live awareness of your broker's state and can take actions on your behalf — no BunkerAI Cloud subscription required.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Local LLM mode is available on all plans, including Community. It uses LM Studio's OpenAI-compatible local server API (http://host.docker.internal:1234/v1 from inside the Docker container) and injects a live broker context snapshot into every chat request as a system prompt.

The feature is functionally equivalent to Cloud AI for the web chat interface. Key differences:

Local LLM Cloud AI (BunkerAI) Cost Free Paid subscription Privacy 100% local — nothing leaves your network Data sent to BunkerAI Cloud Model Your choice (any LM Studio model) Managed by BunkerAI Telegram / Slack Not supported Supported (Pro+) Internet required No Yes

docs.title: Backend (backend/app/agent-api/main.py)
docs.text:
The agent-api service (port 1006) handles all local LLM logic:

  • GET /local-llm/config — Returns current LM Studio URL, selected model, and enabled flag.
  • POST /local-llm/config — Saves configuration to disk (/app/local_llm_config.json).
  • GET /local-llm/models — Proxies GET /v1/models to LM Studio and returns the model list.
  • POST /local-llm/chat — Main chat endpoint. Accepts {messages, model}. Fetches live broker context, builds system prompt, calls LM Studio, parses any BUNKER_ACTION block, executes it, returns the cleaned reply.

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
Three Next.js API routes proxy to the backend with API key injection:

  • app/api/ai/local-llm/config/route.ts — GET/POST config
  • app/api/ai/local-llm/models/route.ts — GET models
  • app/api/ai/local-llm/chat/route.ts — POST chat

Client-side calls are in lib/api.ts under localLlmApi.

docs.title: Broker Context Injection (_fetch_broker_context)
docs.text:
On every chat request, the backend fetches:

  1. Monitor stats (GET :1001/api/v1/stats?nonce=...&timestamp=...) — broker uptime, connected clients, messages/sec, subscriptions.
  2. Active topics (GET :1001/api/v1/topics) — all topics with their latest value.
  3. Connected clients (GET :1002/api/v1/connected-clients) — currently active client IDs.
  4. Registered clients (GET :1000/api/v1/clients) — all DynSec clients.

This context is assembled as a markdown string and injected as the first system message, replacing any previous system message in the history.

docs.title: Action Execution (BUNKER_ACTION protocol)
docs.text:
When the LLM wants to execute an action, it outputs a JSON block in its reply:

BUNKER_ACTION: {"action": "create_client", "username": "sensor-01", "password": "abc123XYZ"}

The backend uses _extract_action_json() (brace-depth character walk, handles nested JSON and code fences) to parse this block, then _execute_action() dispatches:

Action API call create_client POST :1000/api/v1/clients create_clients Loop of POST :1000/api/v1/clients publish POST :1001/api/v1/publish delete_client DELETE :1000/api/v1/clients/{username}

The raw BUNKER_ACTION: block is stripped from the reply before returning it to the user.

docs.title: Step 1 — Start LM Studio Local Server
docs.text:
1. Open LM Studio and go to the Local Server tab.
  2. Load a model (Qwen2.5-Instruct-7B or Llama-3-Instruct recommended).
  3. Click Start Server (default port: 1234).

docs.title: Step 2 — Configure BunkerM
docs.text:
1. In BunkerM, go to Settings → Integrations.
  2. Find the Local LLM card.
  3. Set the URL:
  4. Docker deployment: http://host.docker.internal:1234
  5. Host deployment: http://localhost:1234
  6. Click Fetch Models or toggle Enable (auto-fetches models).
  7. Select your loaded model from the dropdown.
  8. Toggle Enable ON and click Save.

docs.title: Step 3 — Use in Chat
docs.text:
1. Go to AI → Chat.
  2. Click Local LLM in the mode toggle (header).
  3. The selection persists across navigation (stored in localStorage).

docs.title: Chat History & Reset
docs.text:
• Cloud AI and Local LLM histories are stored separately in localStorage:
  • Cloud: bunkerm_chat_{userId}
  • Local LLM: bunkerm_local_llm_chat_{userId}
  • Type /reset in the chat input to clear the current mode's history.
  • Click the trash icon (🗑) in the chat header to clear history.

docs.title: System Prompt
docs.text:
The system prompt (defined in _build_system_prompt()) tells the model:

  • It is BunkerAI, an AI assistant for BunkerM MQTT management.
  • The current broker state (injected live context).
  • The BUNKER_ACTION JSON format and supported actions.
  • Password rules: plain alphanumeric only (a-z, A-Z, 0-9), 12–16 chars — never bcrypt or hashed.
  • Only output a BUNKER_ACTION block when the user explicitly asks to create/delete/publish. Never for read/query/status requests.

docs.title: Supported Models
docs.text:
Any model loaded in LM Studio works. For best results:

  • Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct — strong instruction following, good action execution
  • Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct — fast on CPU, lower RAM
  • Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3 — reliable general purpose

Avoid base (non-instruct) models — they do not follow system prompt instructions reliably.

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
• Telegram and Slack connectors route through BunkerAI Cloud WebSocket and cannot use a local model.
  • Very small models (1–2B) may occasionally generate BUNKER_ACTION blocks for read-only queries despite the system prompt instruction.
  • Local LLM does not have cross-session memory (no shared memory feature like Cloud AI).

docs.title: Shared AI Memory
docs.text: BunkerAI maintains a unified conversation context across all connected channels. Whether you chat in the Web UI, send a message on Slack, or use the Telegram bot, the AI remembers your previous exchanges and your broker's context.

docs.title: What Shared AI Memory Is
docs.text:
When you interact with BunkerAI through any channel, the conversation is stored and associated with your BunkerM instance. This context is available to BunkerAI regardless of which channel the next message comes through.

Shared memory means:

  • A conversation you start in Slack can be continued in Web Chat without repeating yourself
  • Anomaly context you discussed via Telegram is remembered when you open Web Chat
  • Settings and broker topology you have explained in one session carry forward to future sessions
  • The AI does not need to relearn your setup every time you open a new channel

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
All three channels - Web Chat, Slack, and Telegram - connect to the same BunkerAI backend for your BunkerM instance. When you send a message, BunkerAI:

  1. Retrieves your conversation history from the shared store
  2. Includes that history as context in the AI request
  3. Generates a response that is aware of everything previously discussed
  4. Stores the new exchange back in the shared conversation store

The conversation store is per-tenant (per BunkerM instance), not per-channel. There is no separate "Slack history" and "Web Chat history" - there is one unified history for your instance that all channels read from and write to.

docs.title: What Is Remembered
docs.text:
Previous questions and answers - The full text of your previous exchanges is retained and used as context. If you asked "how many clients are in the sensors group?" two days ago, the AI remembers the answer and can refer back to it.

Broker topology you have discussed - When you have explained aspects of your setup (which clients are sensors, which groups exist, what your topic structure means), the AI carries that understanding forward.

Client and topic annotations - Annotations you create in Settings > Annotations are always included in AI context, independently of conversation history. They serve as a persistent knowledge base about your topics.

Actions the AI has taken - If the AI created a client, added a role, or set up a watcher on your behalf in a previous session, it remembers having done that.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
No repetition - You explain your setup once. Whether you return to the Web Chat the next day or message the bot on Telegram a week later, the AI still has context of what you discussed.

Consistent behavior across channels - Switching channels does not change the AI's understanding of your broker. The same knowledge and conversation history is used everywhere.
